title: Comparators and ordering deterministic evidence bank

items.title: Comparators and ordering: reject the invalid rule
items.prompt: Which statement correctly resolves this CPP misconception? A sorting comparator should return true for equal elements so either order is accepted.
hints: Compare each option with the exact language rule, standard boundary, and observable evidence in the authored draft.

feedback.correct: Correct. A strict comparator must return false for equivalent values in both directions; use < semantics and consistent tie-breakers.
feedback.incorrect: That option preserves the documented misconception. A strict comparator must return false for equivalent values in both directions; use < semantics and consistent tie-breakers.

criteria.description: Selects the source-aligned CPP correction instead of the documented misconception.

options.text: A strict comparator must return false for equivalent values in both directions; use < semantics and consistent tie-breakers.
A sorting comparator should return true for equal elements so either order is accepted.

answer.explanation: A strict comparator must return false for equivalent values in both directions; use < semantics and consistent tie-breakers.

items.title: Comparators and ordering: complete the language rule
items.prompt: Complete the missing CPP checkpoint in the source-aligned correction. Use the exact token or phrase required by this draft.
hints: Use the checkpoint that distinguishes the valid rule from this misconception: A sorting comparator should return true for equal elements so either order is accepted.

feedback.correct: Correct. The required checkpoint is 'false for equivalent values'.
feedback.incorrect: Recheck the exact language rule and its boundary. A strict comparator must return false for equivalent values in both directions; use < semantics and consistent tie-breakers.

criteria.description: Supplies the exact CPP token or phrase that completes the reviewed rule candidate.

items.template: A strict comparator must return {{key-rule}} in both directions; use < semantics and consistent tie-breakers.

items.title: Comparators and ordering: executable application
items.prompt: Read name-score records and sort them by descending score then ascending name using a comparator, printing the names in order.

examEligibility.rationale: AI-assisted deterministic draft awaiting independent human standards, security, and assessment review.

hints: Begin with the declared input and exact output contract, then implement the named language facet without relying on undefined behavior.

feedback.correct: The reference-aligned visible and hidden deterministic checks passed in the pinned isolated runtime.
feedback.incorrect: At least one deterministic check failed; inspect input validation, boundary behavior, and the named language rule without exposing hidden cases.

criteria.description: Compiles in the pinned runtime and passes every visible and hidden exact-output case.
Uses the target C or C++ language facet with declared bounds and without an out-of-scope workaround.

privateAuthorNotes: AI-assisted and human-unreviewed: formal exam eligibility is prohibited until independent technical and assessment approval.
Reference verification uses the digest-pinned local container with network=none, no package installation, a read-only source mount, and deterministic stdin/stdout.
Executable facet: strict weak ordering with an explicit tie-breaker

answer.explanation: The reference solution exercises strict weak ordering with an explicit tie-breaker under the bounded deterministic contract; it remains an AI-assisted draft pending human review.
